CHANGELOG.html.title: Changelog
CHANGELOG.html.summary: Changelog All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file. The format is based on Keep a Changelog and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning. [0.1.0] - 2025-07-02 Added Modular locomotion architecture – Introduces a clean separation of concerns by splitting player input, character physics, and the visual model into distinct entities. This design supports both player-controlled and AI-controlled characters, and scales to multiple characters or players in local multiplayer scenarios. State-driven movement system – Character locomotion is governed by a flexible state machine with distinct states (e.g. idle, grounded, jumping, falling, sliding) and smooth automatic transitions between them. Movement behavior is defined by data-driven profiles, making it easy to tune speeds and jump parameters without code changes. Physics-based character controller – Uses physics under the hood for robust movement and collisions. Characters utilize a capsule collider and can step over small obstacles up to a defined height, as well as traverse slopes up to a maximum angle before sliding down steeper inclines. The system handles gravity and falling, inherits movement from moving platforms (so characters ride platforms smoothly), and includes solid collision resolution to prevent getting stuck on edges or corners. Integrated input handling – Built-in support for player input, character movement direction is calculated relative to the camera’s orientation, and an optional “aim” input allows the character to align with the camera direction for intuitive control. Input capture uses the new input system for easily supporting multiple control devices. Camera tracking for characters – A camera target tracking system automatically follows player characters to keep them in view. Integration with the new input system and PlayerInputManager allows natural support for split screen local multiplayer.The camera target follows smoothly the position and rotation in both single-player and local multiplayer scenarios. The solution supports use of Cinemachine cameras. Animation-ready character models – Visual presentation is decoupled from logic by spawning a separate model GameObject for rendering and animation. The locomotion system keeps the model’s transform in sync with the character’s state, enabling developers to hook up animations (e.g. running, jumping) to the character’s movement without affecting the underlying physics simulation. Customizable locomotion profiles – Character movement characteristics are driven by scriptable Locomotion Profile assets. These profiles define parameters like movement speed, acceleration, turning rate, allowed jump count, jump heights, and gravity settings for each character. This allows easy tweaking of locomotion behavior and per-character tuning by adjusting profile values in the editor. Example scenes – Included sample scenes demonstrate the locomotion system in action. A single-player example and a local multiplayer example are provided, showcasing basic player movement, jumping, camera follow, and multiple characters controlled simultaneously.

api/Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring.BaseCha.summary: Class BaseCharacterAuthoring<TProfile>.BaseCharacterBaker<TAuthoring, TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TInput, TStateData> Baker for the CharacterAuthoring. Converts the authoring data into one or more ECS entities with the following setup: Creates the primary character entity (manual‐override transform) and, if Playable is checked, adds a PlayableCharacter tag. If a ModelPrefab is assigned, spawns a separate “model” entity with: – PresentationGo (holds the prefab for rendering) – ControllingEntity (points back to the character entity) Applies the authoring transform to the character via LocalTransform. If a CharacterProfile is provided: – Generates or reuses a BlobAssetReference from the profile asset (and calls DependsOn so changes re-bake automatically) – Adds LocomotionInput to drive character movement from player input or AI Agent – Builds a CapsuleCollider from profile settings (CapsuleGeometry + CollisionFilter) and adds it as a PhysicsCollider blob asset – Registers the character with the physics world (PhysicsWorldIndex) – Adds LocomotionProfileReference (points to the blob asset), LocomotionData (initial velocity, jump count, etc.), LocomotionGravity (gravity multiplier) – api/Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring.BaseCha.title: Class BaseCharacterAuthoring<TProfile>
api/Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring.BaseCha.summary: Class BaseCharacterAuthoring<TProfile> Serves as a base class for authoring character entities with locomotion capabilities. Designed to work with a profile of type TProfile, enabling customization of character movement and behavior through specific profile definitions. Contains shared properties and functionality for setting up entities related to character locomotion, including rendering models and defining state machines through further specialization. The class includes a nested abstract baker class used for converting authoring data into ECS entities with the following configurations: Associates the authored character with a profile asset (defining locomotion behavior and rules). Supports optional assignment of a prefab model entity for visual representation. Facilitates the integration of locomotion state machines, states, and custom contexts specific to the movement profile. api/Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring.html.title: Namespace Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring
api/Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring.html.summary: Namespace Locomotion.Runtime.Authoring Classes BaseCharacterAuthoring<TProfile> Serves as a base class for authoring character entities with locomotion capabilities. Designed to work with a profile of type TProfile, enabling customization of character movement and behavior through specific profile definitions. Contains shared properties and functionality for setting up entities related to character locomotion, including rendering models and defining state machines through further specialization. The class includes a nested abstract baker class used for converting authoring data into ECS entities with the following configurations: Associates the authored character with a profile asset (defining locomotion behavior and rules). Supports optional assignment of a prefab model entity for visual representation. Facilitates the integration of locomotion state machines, states, and custom contexts specific to the movement profile. BaseCharacterAuthoring<TProfile>.BaseCharacterBaker<TAuthoring, TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TInput, TStateData> Baker for the CharacterAuthoring. Converts the authoring data into one or more ECS entities with the following setup: Creates the primary character entity (manual‐override transform) and, if Playable is checked, adds a PlayableCharacter tag. If a ModelPrefab is assigned, spawns a separate “model” entity with: – PresentationGo (holds the prefab for rendering) – ControllingEntity (points back to the character entity) Applies the authoring transform to the character via LocalTransform. If a CharacterProfile is provided: – Generates or reuses a BlobAssetReference from the profile asset (and calls DependsOn so changes re-bake automatically) – Adds LocomotionInput to drive character movement from player input or AI Agent – Builds a CapsuleCollider from profile settings (CapsuleGeometry + CollisionFilter) and adds it as a PhysicsCollider blob asset – Registers the character with the physics world (PhysicsWorldIndex) – Adds LocomotionProfileReference (points to the blob asset), LocomotionData (initial velocity, jump count, etc.), LocomotionGravity (gravity multiplier) – Initializes the CharacterLocomotionStateMachine in the Falling state

api/Locomotion.Runtime.Components.html.title: Namespace Locomotion.Runtime.Components
api/Locomotion.Runtime.Components.html.summary: Namespace Locomotion.Runtime.Components Structs Character Represents a character within the locomotion system. The Character struct includes references to the locomotion and model sub-entities associated with the character. Typically used to manage and query the state of characters within gameplay systems, ensuring updates to locomotion, transformations, and other related components are synchronized. CharacterPath A list of transform teh character went through. The first element is the next target position and the second and third element are previous transforms. Time allows for interpolation between the previous position and next target position. The third element can be used to compute velocity and acceleration. CharacterPhysicsProfile Represents a physics configuration profile for a character, encapsulating core properties that define collision behavior, dimensions, and interaction with physics layers. Contact Information about a collision contact point. ControlledCharacters List of character entity controlled by the player or AI Agent LocomotionContact Last ground or obstacle contact information. LocomotionGravity Encapsulates gravity settings for the character. This value can be updated by a system to simulate any arbitrary gravitiy direction. LocomotionVelocity Locomotion velocity for a character locomotor. Platform PlayerGameObject Set of references to mono objects. These are used to update and managed game object behaviors of the player entity from systems allowing for greater control on the player loop. PlayerGameObjectPrefab Set of references to mono objects. These are used to update and managed game object behaviors of the player entity from systems allowing for greater control on the player loop. Rotor Track TrackedMotion Tracks a transform over time to compute inherited movement and velocity.

manual/Design & Architecture/Main Concep.title: Generics and Source Generator
manual/Design & Architecture/Main Concep.summary: Generics and Source Generator This package relies on generic types and source generators to reduce the boilerplate code needed to implement the character locomotion. Understanding the which generic types exists and what is source generated is key for your comprehension of the package inner working and to enable customisation fo the behavior. Generics TStateMachine: The type of the locomotion state machine itself, which implements this interface. TStates: The type representing the collection of states managed by the state machine. TContext: The type of the context object that provides state, input data, and profile information necessary for the locomotion logic. TProfile: The type of the locomotion profile containing configuration settings specific to the locomotion logic. TInput: The type of the input data influencing locomotion, such as user inputs or triggers. TStateData: The type of the state data component holding state specific information. Source Generated Content All source generated content is generated from your list of states. For the generation to work the enum need to be marked with the attribute LocomotionStateMachine. Each enum value needs to be marked with the LocomotionState(TStates) attribute. All generated types are named using the concrete type of TStates as a prefix. As part of this documentation they will be reference using the #TStates#. All generated types are generated as partial types to allow their extension. StateMachine The state machine is source generated by picking up the LocomotionStateMachine. As part of the attribute you have to define your specific implementation of the generic types TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData. This allows the source generator to create a partial struct to hold the current state of the state machine : public partial struct #TStates#StateMachine : IComponentData,ILocomotionStateMachine<#TStates#StateMachine, #TStates#LocomotionStates, #TStates#Context, #TStates#Profile, #TStates#Input, #TStates#StateData> For each of the method defined by the interface, the generator will implement switch statements from the enum values marked with LocomotionState(TStates) attribute. The generated #TState#StateMachine will be added to the character entity by the baking system and stores the current state (TState) of the state machine. Input reader system Using the #TState#InputReaderSystem TInput defined in the LocomotionStateMachine attribute, the pâckage will generate two input systems : PlayerInputManagerSystem : in charge of initialising the player input asset and capturing the player input from the asset public partial struct #TState#PlayerInputManagerSystem : ISystem InputReaderSystem : in charge of applying the player input to the player controlled characters public partial struct #TState#InputReaderSystem : ISystem Movement System This is the system that schedule the execution of the state machine. For that it uses all the generic types defined by the LocomotionStateMachine attribute to generate a ISystem: public partial struct BasicLocomotionStatesLocomotionSystem : ISystem

manual/Design & Architecture/Main Concep.title: Player
manual/Design & Architecture/Main Concep.summary:
Player As explained in the package overview, a Player represents the person interacting with the game, typically through an input device (keyboard, gamepad, etc.). Following the hybrid approach (integrating GameObjects with Entities), the Player is represented both as a GameObject and as an Entity. The Player GameObject The Player GameObject must be a prefab with a Player GO Authoring component at its root. This component: Holds references to the player’s camera and its camera target (often an empty GameObject under the prefab root). Defines the camera follow strategy for controlled characters. Beyond this requirement, the Player GameObject can have any structure and use any input or camera management system you prefer. Baking into the Player Entity The Player GO Authoring component must be referenced on a Player Character Authoring component, placed in a sub-scene. During baking, the Player GameObject prefab is stored in the PlayerGameObjectPrefab component of the Player entity. At runtime, the PlayerGoManagerSystem instantiates and initializes the prefab. The instantiated GameObject is then accessible through the PlayerGameObjectInstance component on the Player entity. Input Assets The Player Character Authoring also requires a reference to a PlayerInputAsset, which defines how player input is read to construct the character’s TInput. This asset can include: Inspector references (e.g., Input Action mappings). Script references, via the method: PlayerInputAsset.InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance playerGameObject) This lets you cache references to MonoBehaviours such as cameras, Cinemachine brains, or PlayerInput components. Multiple players can reference the same PlayerInputAsset. Like the GameObject prefab, the asset itself is not used at runtime. Instead: The source-generated system #TStates#PlayerInputManagerSystem duplicates the asset. It then calls InitInputAsset(...), passing the Player GameObject instance. Finally, the copy is stored in the Player entity as PlayerInputInstance. The Player Entity In the hybrid approach, the preferred way to interact with MonoBehaviours is through systems. This ensures proper update order between ECS systems and MonoBehaviour updates—crucial for smooth camera tracking and character control. Accessing the GameObject Use the PlayerGameObjectInstance component. You can query this component from jobs or Burst functions, but direct GameObject access must occur on the main thread in a non-Burst context. Accessing Input Use the PlayerInputInstance component. The same restrictions apply: querying can be Burst, but using the input asset must be done on the main thread. Controlled Characters The Player entity also has a DynamicBuffer that stores references to the entities of all controlled characters. This buffer is used by: CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem — updates the camera target based on the follow strategy defined in the Player GO Authoring component. #TStates#InputReaderSystem — reads input from PlayerInputInstance and applies it to all controlled characters by calling: TInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter(TInput) Controlled characters can be assigned: In the inspector, via the Player Character Authoring. At runtime, by adding entries to the DynamicBuffer. manual/Design & Architecture/Main Concep.title: State Machine
manual/Design & Architecture/Main Concep.summary: State Machine The Locomotion package uses a state-machine-based implementation to handle character movement behavior. A typical state machine uses three lifecycle methods: OnEnterState — runs when entering a new state. OnUpdateState — runs every frame while in a state. OnExitState — runs when leaving a state. For example, when transitioning from a falling state to a grounded state, you might nullify the character’s vertical velocity inside OnEnterState or OnExitState. In our character controller, however, many operations must occur in a strict order, regardless of the current state. To handle this, the state machine provides a default execution loop via the interface: ILocomotionStateMachine<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> This loop runs common state-independent logic automatically. For state-specific behavior, each state must implement the interface: ILocomotionState<TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> To override the default resolution loop, see Overriding State Machine Behavior. Movement and Collision Resolution The package relies on the Collide and Slide algorithm to handle character movement and collision resolution. Each state defines two core methods: OnIntegrateState — calculates the intended displacement for this frame and updates velocity based on input and external forces (e.g., gravity). Called once per fixed update. OnResolveStateCollisions — applies the movement, performing collision detection and sliding along surfaces when needed. How OnResolveStateCollisions works Read the displacement computed by OnIntegrateState. Project that displacement onto the last known collision surface. Perform a physics cast along this path. If a collision occurs: Move the character to the collision point. Compute the remaining displacement. Repeat until all displacement is resolved or a maximum number of iterations is reached. This iterative collision resolution loop is handled automatically by the state machine. You only need to: Define how sliding is computed. Perform collision checks. The package provides helper methods in PhysicsOperations for collision detection and displacement computation. Note: State changes can occur between iterations. For example, if the falling state detects ground mid-displacement, the state machine will: Move the character to the ground using the falling state. Switch to the grounded state. Continue resolving the remaining displacement using the grounded state. Platform Management Characters can also be moved by external sources such as moving platforms. During each fixed update, the state machine computes any inherited platform velocity/displacement before calling OnIntegrateState. The PhysicsOperations utilities automatically factor this displacement into collision resolution. You should not add it manually in OnIntegrateState. However, you can project it in OnResolveStateCollisions like you would for player-driven displacement. To enable platform tracking, add the PlatformTrackerAuthoring component to the moving object. This automatically computes its velocity each fixed update based on position changes. Overlap Resolution In many games, collisions are not only caused by the character’s own movement but also by external entities (e.g., moving platforms, falling objects, or other characters). To handle this, the state machine performs overlap resolution as the first step of its execution loop: It checks for overlapping colliders using overlap tests. If overlaps exist, it attempts to separate the character iteratively. The process continues until the character is no longer overlapping or a maximum number of iterations is reached. This ensures characters can react properly to being pushed or intersected by other moving entities. Ground Snapping Sometimes characters unintentionally lift off the ground, such as when moving over a slope that suddenly flattens or reverses. This may cause jittery movement or trigger incorrect animation transitions (e.g., briefly switching to a “falling” state). To prevent this, the state machine performs ground snapping as its final step: A downward physics cast is performed in the gravity direction. If the ground is within a certain threshold, the character is snapped to it. The last ground contact is stored for use in the next frame’s collision resolution. Ground snapping details The default snapping distance is set to the character’s shell width. You can override this by implementing ComputeGroundSnappingDistance in your custom ILocomotionContext<TProfile, TInput, TStateData>. Ground snapping only occurs if the character was grounded earlier in the same fixed update (to avoid sticking during jumps or early snapping when falling). Overriding State Machine Behavior The package is designed to be extensible. States are implemented as structs with static methods. These are executed in a Burst-compatible context. A source generator automatically creates the boilerplate state machine logic (switch statements, method calls, etc.). Because the generated code isn’t directly in your source, it might seem unmodifiable—but you can override the execution loop: Declare a partial struct with the same name as your state enum, suffixed with StateMachine. public partial struct #TStates#StateMachine : IComponentData {} You may need to redeclare the IComponentData interface for the compiler to not throw an error. Implement your own execution logic by overriding: ILocomotionStateMachine<TStateMachine, TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> .Execute(ref TStateMachine stateMachine, ref TContext context) This lets you fully customize the state machine’s execution loop while keeping the package’s performance and Burst compatibility benefits. Thus ensuring the character remains correctly attached to dynamic surfaces."] end Integrate --> Collisions["Resolve collisions"] Collisions --> EvaluateTransition["Evaluate Transition"] subgraph s2[" "] EvaluateTransition["Evaluate Transition"] CommentEvaluateTransition["After each collision resolution pass, invokes the state machine’s transition logic. manual/Design & Architecture/Systems/Cha.title: Previewing the Character in Editor
manual/Design & Architecture/Systems/Cha.summary: Previewing the Character in Editor When designing your game you will have to place character in the world. Since the hybrid workflow forces us to rely on prefab that are spawned at runtime rather than placed directly in the scene, it can be difficult to see where and which characters we are placing in the scene. For this reason the EntityGoEditorPreviewSystem was created to display in editor only any of the Entity having a GameObject representation. It does not instantiate the GameObject but rather extract all its rendering data to display it on screen at every Editor Update.

manual/Design & Architecture/Systems/Inp.title: Input Management
manual/Design & Architecture/Systems/Inp.summary:
Input Management When dealing with input in a game, especially when integrating character movement with physics simulation, it's essential to distinguish between player input (input device data) and character input (simulation-ready data). Additionally, understanding the timing differences between frame updates and fixed updates is crucial for reliable input handling. Player Input vs Character Input Player Input refers to raw data from input devices like keyboard, mouse, or gamepad. These inputs are read every frame, typically on the main thread during Update(). Character Input is derived from player input and is used to drive the character’s behavior during the physics-based simulation. Since our character simulation aligns with the physics simulation, this data is processed during FixedUpdate, making it subject to a different time step than the player input. Fixed Time step & Input Handling What Is a Fixed Time step? Unity’s ECS FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup ensures that systems update at consistent intervals (e.g., 60Hz). This is essential for simulations requiring determinism, such as physics or server-side logic. However, fixed updates don’t necessarily align 1:1 with regular frame updates: High Frame Rate: You may get multiple regular updates per fixed update. Low Frame Rate: You may get multiple fixed updates per regular update. This means: In one rendered frame, there might be zero, one, or many fixed updates. Input captured per-frame might not sync correctly with input consumed per-fixed-update. Common Issues with Input in Fixed Updates When input is processed in a fixed time step context, two main problems can occur: Missed Inputs (High Frame Rate) Input events that occur between fixed updates may be lost if they aren't persisted until the next fixed update. Repeated Inputs (Low Frame Rate) If multiple fixed updates occur between two regular updates, the same input state may be consumed multiple times, causing repeated actions. For example, with a jump input: In the first case, the character might fail to jump. In the second, it might jump twice or higher than intended. sequenceDiagram participant Device as 🕹️ Player Input Device participant Player as 🔄 Player Input participant Character as ⚙️ Character Input %% High FPS - Input Missed Note over Player, Character: 🟥 Case 1: High Frame Rate - Input Missed rect rgb(122,122,122) Note right of Device: Frame 1 Player->>Device: ReadInput() → ✅ Jump pressed! Note right of Player: Input = true
No FixedUpdate this frame end rect rgb(122,122,122) Note right of Device: Frame 2 Player->>Device: ReadInput() → ❌ (no input) Player->>Character: ApplyPlayerInput() → Jump = false Character-->>Character: SimulateCharacter()
⛔ Input missed end %% Divider Note over Player, Character: 🟧 Case 2: Low Frame Rate - Input Consumed Twice rect rgb(122,122,122) Note right of Device: Frame 1 Player->>Device: ReadInput() → ✅ Jump pressed! Player->>Character: ApplyPlayerInput() → Jump = true Note right of Character: 1st fixed update Character-->>Character: SimulateCharacter() → Jump = true ✅ Note right of Character: 2nd fixed update Character-->>Character: SimulateCharacter() → Jump = true ❗ (again) Note right of Character: ⚠️ Jump consumed twice end Recommended Input Handling Strategy To ensure robust input handling across all frame rates, use the following strategy: Accumulate trigger-based inputs (e.g., button presses) between frames. Consume these inputs once per simulation cycle (usually inside the locomotion state machine). Reset or clear the accumulated trigger after consumption. sequenceDiagram participant Device as 🕹️ Player Input Device participant Player as 🔄 Player Input (Update) participant Character as ⚙️ Character Input (FixedUpdate) Note over Device, Character: ✅ Fixed Input Handling with Accumulation - High Frame Rate rect rgb(122,122,122) Note right of Device: Frame 1 Player->>Device: ReadInput() → ✅ Jump pressed! Player->>Character: AccumulateInput(jump=1) Note right of Character: Character Input

✅ Input caught Character->>-Character: ConsumeJumpTrigger() Note right of Character: Character Input

✅ Input not consumed twice Character->>-Character: ConsumeJumpTrigger() end To facilitate this, the input system defines two key concepts: Character Input Struct Implements ICharacterLocomotionInput . Represents simulation-ready data (e.g., movement direction, jump trigger). public struct YourLocomotionInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput { public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in YourLocomotionInput playerInputs) { } } Player Input Asset A ScriptableObject derived from PlayerInputAsset . Maps raw device inputs into a character input struct. [Serializable] [CreateAssetMenu(fileName = "YourCharacterProfileAsset", menuName = "ScriptableObjects/WAYN Games/Your Player Character Input Asset", order = 1)] public class YourPlayerCharacterInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset { [...] public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out YourLocomotionInput inputs) { [...] } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObject playerGameObject) { [...] } } Based on the above types, the package uses source generator to create the following runtime systems: PlayerInputManagerSystem Instantiates and initializes the PlayerInputAsset for each player. Calls InitInputAsset, allowing setup of player-specific references (e.g., camera, Unity’s PlayerInput component). [Serializable] [CreateAssetMenu(fileName = "YourCharacterProfileAsset", menuName = "ScriptableObjects/WAYN Games/Your Player Character Input Asset", order = 1)] public class YourPlayerCharacterInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset { /// /// Reference to the player's camera used for determining movement directions /// and input in relation to the camera's perspective in the game world. /// private Camera m_PlayerCamera; public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out YourLocomotionInput inputs) { [...] } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObject playerGameObject) { // Get the camera from the player game object and cache it for use when reading inputs. m_PlayerCamera = playerGameObject.Instance.Value.GetComponent().Camera; } } InputReaderSystem Runs every frame (Update) on the main thread. Calls ReadInputsFromAsset(out TInput input) to convert player input into a TInput struct (character input). ⚠️ Important: ReadInputsFromAsset() runs on the main thread and must not be Burst-compiled. It may access managed Unity types such as Camera, Input, or MonoBehaviour components. Use this method only for device-to-character input mapping, not for simulation logic. [Serializable] [CreateAssetMenu(fileName = "YourCharacterProfileAsset", menuName = "ScriptableObjects/WAYN Games/Your Player Character Input Asset", order = 1)] public class YourPlayerCharacterInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset { /// /// Reference to the player's camera used for determining movement directions /// and input in relation to the camera's perspective in the game world. /// private Camera m_PlayerCamera; public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out YourLocomotionInput inputs) { Transform cameraTransform = m_PlayerCamera.transform; float3 cameraSpaceMovement = cameraTransform.forward * Input.GetAxis("Vertical") + cameraTransform.right * Input.GetAxis("Horizontal"); float3 groundSpaceMovement = cameraSpaceMovement - math.projectsafe(cameraSpaceMovement, math.up()); inputs.Movement = math.normalizesafe(groundSpaceMovement); inputs.JumpTrigger = Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Space) ? 1 : 0; } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObject playerGameObject) { [...] } } Once the input is read, the system schedules a job that: Loops over all player-controlled characters. Calls ApplyInputsToCharacter() on the character input struct. This is where trigger inputs must be accumulated to ensure consistent behavior across frame rates. /// /// Input commands for the characters' locomotion. /// public struct YourLocomotionInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput { /// /// Represents the directional movement input vector for the locomotion system. /// This vector is used to define the desired movement direction and magnitude /// for a character, typically based on player input, such as keyboard or joystick controls. /// public float3 Movement; /// /// Represents the input trigger value for initiating a jump action in the locomotion system. /// This value is accumulated over time to account for frame rate differences and indicates /// whether a jump has been requested by the player. /// public float JumpTrigger; [...] public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in YourLocomotionInput playerInputs) { Movement = playerInputs.Movement; // Trigger inputs need to be accumulated to account for frame rate difference between input capture (update) and consumption (fixed updated) JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger; } } To use your input in your state machine, you can access and consume the character input through the LocomotionContext internal static void OnIntegrateState(ref YourLocomotionContext ctx) { // Check if jump was triggered if (ctx.InputValue.JumpTrigger > 0) { // Perform Jump // DoJump() // Consume the jump trigger immediately after jump has been initiated ConsumeJumpTrigger(ref ctx); } } private static void ConsumeJumpTrigger(ref YourLocomotionContext ctx) { YourLocomotionInput value = ctx.InputValue; value.JumpTrigger = 0; ctx.InputValue = value; }

manual/Overview.html.title: Locomotion Package Overview
manual/Overview.html.summary: Locomotion Package Overview The Locomotion Package provides a modular, ECS-based architecture for implementing character control in Unity using state-driven locomotion logic. It cleanly separates gameplay entities—Players, Characters, and Models—and handles input, movement, and visual synchronization in a scalable and maintainable way. It integrates seamlessly with Unity's New Input System and Cinemachine, but remains flexible enough to support custom input or camera solutions. Core Concepts Player vs Character The concepts of Player and Character are treated as distinct: Player: Represents the person interacting with the game, typically through an input device (keyboard, gamepad, etc.). Character: An in-game entity that responds to the player's commands, or may be AI-controlled. This separation allows for flexible gameplay mechanics, such as: Players switching between characters Players controlling multiple characters simultaneously Characters being controlled by AI logic instead of a player Each concept has its own lifecycle and responsibilities within the simulation. Character : Simulation vs Presentation Every Character is internally divided into two subsystems: Simulation: Responsible for physics, movement, and game logic. This part processes input and updates the character’s state deterministically in the ECS world. Presentation: Responsible for visuals, including animations, mesh rendering, and audio. It visually reflects the state determined by the simulation. 💡 Why this matters (especially for multiplayer): In networked or server-authoritative games, simulation often occurs on the server, while the presentation remains client-side. Decoupling these concerns allows for: Smooth local visuals (even with lag) Efficient bandwidth usage Support for prediction and interpolation Better separation between gameplay code and rendering logic GameObjects vs Entities While Unity ECS is production-ready, several important features—especially Input, Camera, and Animation—still rely on GameObjects. As such, this package uses a hybrid architecture, where both ECS entities and GameObjects coexist. How the hybrid structure works: Player Entity side: Controls logic, such as tracking which characters the player is managing. GameObject side: Handles input (via PlayerInput) and camera (via Cinemachine). Character Locomotor Entity: Simulates physics and processes state-driven movement. Model Entity: Follows the locomotor entity using interpolation, ensuring smooth visuals between fixed updates. GameObject Model: Manages skinned mesh rendering, animation, and audio components. This separation allows the simulation systems to remain lightweight, jobified, and Burst-compatible, while the visual systems leverage the strengths of GameObject-based components. If you encounter issues or have suggestions, please contact: Email to contact@wayn.games Please include the package version and a clear description of the issue or suggestion.
